q: Summarize the book of Joshua: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Traditionally Joshua himself (with later editorial notes, e.g. his death account); c. 1400 B.C.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** God faithfully fulfills His covenant promise to give Israel the land of Canaan, granting victory through obedient faith and apportioning the inheritance among the tribes.

**Outline.**
- 1–5 — Entering the land: Jordan crossing, circumcision, Passover
- 6–12 — Conquest of Canaan: Jericho, Ai, southern and northern campaigns
- 13–21 — Division of the land among the twelve tribes
- 22–24 — Tribal disputes resolved; Joshua's farewell and covenant renewal

**Christ & significance.** Joshua (Hebrew *Yeshua*) prefigures Jesus who leads His people into the true rest; the conquered inheritance anticipates the eternal kingdom secured by Christ (Heb. 4).
summary: Traditionally Joshua, c. 1400 B.C. God keeps His covenant by giving Israel the land: entry (1–5), conquest (6–12), division (13–21), and covenant renewal (22–24).

q: Summarize the book of Judges: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Traditionally attributed to Samuel; composed c. 1050–1000 B.C., recounting events from roughly 1380–1050 B.C.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** Israel's recurring cycle of apostasy, oppression, repentance, and deliverance shows that without a faithful king and covenant obedience, the nation spirals into chaos — "everyone did what was right in his own eyes."

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Incomplete conquest and the downward cycle introduced
- 3–16 — The judges: Othniel, Ehud, Deborah, Gideon, Jephthah, Samson, and others
- 17–18 — Religious corruption: Micah's idols and the Danite migration
- 19–21 — Moral collapse: the outrage at Gibeah and civil war

**Christ & significance.** The judges are flawed, temporary deliverers whose failures heighten the longing for a righteous, permanent King — fulfilled in Christ, the true Deliverer and Judge.
summary: Traditionally Samuel, c. 1050–1000 B.C. Israel cycles through sin, oppression, and rescue by judges; their flaws expose the need for the righteous King fulfilled in Christ.

q: Summarize the book of Ruth: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Anonymous; traditionally attributed to Samuel; written c. 1010–1000 B.C., set during the period of the judges.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** Amid the dark era of the judges, God's faithful providence and covenant loyalty (*hesed*) preserve a family and weave a Gentile widow into the lineage of David and the Messiah.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Tragedy in Moab: Naomi's loss and Ruth's loyalty
- 2 — Ruth gleans in the field of Boaz
- 3 — Ruth seeks Boaz as kinsman-redeemer at the threshing floor
- 4 — Boaz redeems; the line leading to David

**Christ & significance.** Boaz the kinsman-redeemer foreshadows Christ, our Redeemer; the genealogy closing the book (Obed–Jesse–David) advances the Davidic covenant toward Jesus.
summary: Anonymous (traditionally Samuel), c. 1010 B.C. God's providence and Boaz the kinsman-redeemer carry Ruth into David's line, picturing Christ our Redeemer.

q: Summarize the book of 1 Samuel: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Compiled from records of Samuel, Nathan, and Gad (1 Chron. 29:29); reaching final form c. 930 B.C., covering events c. 1100–1010 B.C.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** The transition from judges to monarchy: God establishes kingship in Israel, rejecting Saul for his disobedience and choosing David, a man after His own heart.

**Outline.**
- 1–7 — Samuel: birth, call, and ministry as prophet-judge
- 8–15 — Saul: Israel demands a king; Saul's rise and rejection
- 16–20 — David anointed and rising; conflict with Saul begins
- 21–31 — David the fugitive; Saul's decline and death at Gilboa

**Christ & significance.** David, the anointed king after God's heart, prefigures Christ the true Anointed One (Messiah); the rejection of self-willed Saul underscores the need for God's chosen King.
summary: Compiled from prophetic records (final form c. 930 B.C.). Samuel anoints kings; Saul is rejected and David chosen as the anointed king who points to Christ.

q: Summarize the book of 2 Samuel: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Compiled from prophetic records (Samuel, Nathan, Gad); final form c. 930 B.C., covering David's reign c. 1010–970 B.C.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** The reign of King David — his triumphs, his great sin, and its consequences — centered on God's covenant to establish David's throne forever.

**Outline.**
- 1–4 — David mourns Saul and reigns over Judah
- 5–10 — David unites Israel, takes Jerusalem; the Davidic covenant (ch. 7)
- 11–12 — David's sin with Bathsheba and Nathan's rebuke
- 13–20 — Family and national turmoil: Absalom's rebellion
- 21–24 — Appendix: songs, exploits, and the census

**Christ & significance.** The Davidic covenant (7:12–16), promising an eternal throne to David's offspring, is the redemptive-historical backbone fulfilled in Jesus, the Son of David who reigns forever.
summary: Prophetic records, final form c. 930 B.C. David's reign and his sin unfold under the Davidic covenant (ch. 7), whose eternal throne is fulfilled in Christ.

q: Summarize the book of 1 Kings: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Anonymous; traditionally attributed to Jeremiah or a prophetic compiler; written during the exile, c. 560–550 B.C., covering c. 970–850 B.C.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** Solomon's glory and the temple give way to the kingdom's division and decline, as kings are measured by covenant faithfulness and the rise of prophets like Elijah confronts apostasy.

**Outline.**
- 1–11 — Solomon: accession, wisdom, temple-building, and final apostasy
- 12–14 — The kingdom divides: Jeroboam (north) and Rehoboam (south)
- 15–16 — Successive kings of Israel and Judah
- 17–22 — Elijah versus Ahab and the prophets of Baal

**Christ & significance.** Solomon's temple and wisdom foreshadow Christ, the greater Solomon and true temple; the kings' failures sharpen the hope for the righteous Davidic King to come.
summary: Anonymous (traditionally Jeremiah), exilic c. 560 B.C. Solomon builds the temple, the kingdom divides, and Elijah confronts apostasy — all pointing to the greater Son of David.

q: Summarize the book of 2 Kings: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Anonymous; traditionally attributed to Jeremiah or a prophetic compiler; written during the exile, c. 560–550 B.C., covering c. 850–586 B.C.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** The persistent covenant unfaithfulness of Israel and Judah brings the LORD's judgment, culminating in the fall of both kingdoms and the Babylonian exile, vindicating the covenant's warnings.

**Outline.**
- 1–8 — Elisha's ministry and miracles
- 9–17 — Decline and fall of the northern kingdom to Assyria (722 B.C.)
- 18–20 — Hezekiah's reforms and deliverance of Jerusalem
- 21–23 — Manasseh's evil and Josiah's reforms
- 24–25 — Judah's fall and the Babylonian exile (586 B.C.)

**Christ & significance.** Exile shows that sin forfeits the inheritance, yet the preserved Davidic line (Jehoiachin's release, 25:27–30) keeps alive the messianic hope realized in Christ.
summary: Anonymous (traditionally Jeremiah), exilic c. 560 B.C. Elisha's ministry, then the fall of Israel and Judah into exile; the surviving Davidic line preserves messianic hope.

q: Summarize the book of 1 Chronicles: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Traditionally Ezra; written c. 450–430 B.C. for the post-exilic community.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** Retelling Israel's history through a priestly, Davidic lens, Chronicles reassures the returned exiles of their identity, the centrality of true worship, and God's enduring covenant with David.

**Outline.**
- 1–9 — Genealogies from Adam through the tribes and returnees
- 10 — Saul's death sets the stage
- 11–12 — David's rise and mighty men
- 13–16 — The ark brought to Jerusalem; worship established
- 17 — The Davidic covenant reaffirmed
- 18–29 — David's reign, preparations for the temple, and Solomon's succession

**Christ & significance.** The emphasis on David's throne and temple worship orients the post-exilic hope toward the coming Son of David, Jesus, the true King and builder of God's house.
summary: Traditionally Ezra, c. 450 B.C. Genealogies and David's reign retold for returned exiles, stressing the Davidic covenant and worship that point to Christ.

q: Summarize the book of 2 Chronicles: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Traditionally Ezra; written c. 450–430 B.C. for the post-exilic community.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** Tracing the kings of Judah from Solomon to the exile, Chronicles highlights the temple, true worship, and that the nation's welfare hinges on covenant faithfulness — closing with hope in the decree to return.

**Outline.**
- 1–9 — Solomon's reign and the building of the temple
- 10–12 — The division and Rehoboam's reign
- 13–28 — The kings of Judah: reforms and apostasies
- 29–32 — Hezekiah's reforms and revival
- 33–36 — Decline, Josiah's reforms, the exile, and Cyrus's decree to return

**Christ & significance.** The temple-centered worship and the preserved Davidic line look forward to Christ, the true temple and King; the closing decree (36:22–23) keeps the redemptive promise open.
summary: Traditionally Ezra, c. 450 B.C. Judah's kings from Solomon's temple to exile, ending with Cyrus's decree to return — worship and David's line pointing to Christ.

q: Summarize the book of Ezra: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Traditionally Ezra the priest-scribe; written c. 440–430 B.C., recounting events from 538 B.C. onward.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** God sovereignly restores His covenant people from Babylonian exile, rebuilding the temple and reforming the community around His law and pure worship.

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Cyrus's decree and the return under Zerubbabel
- 3–6 — Rebuilding the temple amid opposition; its completion
- 7–8 — Ezra's return with a second group of exiles
- 9–10 — Ezra's reform: confession and the issue of mixed marriages

**Christ & significance.** The restored temple and renewed community anticipate the greater restoration in Christ, who gathers and purifies a people for God and is Himself the true temple.
summary: Traditionally Ezra, c. 440 B.C. God restores the exiles, the temple is rebuilt, and Ezra reforms worship and the law — foreshadowing restoration in Christ.

q: Summarize the book of Nehemiah: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Drawn largely from Nehemiah's own memoirs, traditionally compiled by Ezra; written c. 430–420 B.C.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** Through prayerful, courageous leadership, Nehemiah rebuilds Jerusalem's walls and, with Ezra, leads the people in covenant renewal, securing the restored community against internal and external threats.

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Nehemiah's prayer and commission to rebuild
- 3–7 — Rebuilding the wall despite opposition
- 8–10 — Reading of the Law, confession, and covenant renewal
- 11–13 — Repopulating Jerusalem and Nehemiah's final reforms

**Christ & significance.** The rebuilt city and renewed covenant point toward the secure, holy people God forms in Christ, who builds the true Jerusalem and writes His law on the heart.
summary: From Nehemiah's memoirs (traditionally compiled by Ezra), c. 430 B.C. Jerusalem's walls are rebuilt and the covenant renewed under the Law, anticipating God's work in Christ.

q: Summarize the book of Esther: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Anonymous; written c. 460–440 B.C., set in the Persian court of Xerxes (Ahasuerus), c. 483–473 B.C.

**Genre.** Narrative / History.

**Theme.** Though God is never named, His hidden providence preserves the Jewish people from annihilation through Esther's courage and Mordecai's faithfulness, grounding the feast of Purim.

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Esther becomes queen; Mordecai uncovers a plot
- 3–4 — Haman's plot to destroy the Jews; "for such a time as this"
- 5–7 — Esther's intervention and Haman's downfall
- 8–10 — The Jews' deliverance and the institution of Purim

**Christ & significance.** God's unseen providence in preserving His covenant people safeguards the line through which Messiah comes; Esther's mediating intercession faintly pictures Christ's saving advocacy.
summary: Anonymous, c. 460 B.C. In the Persian court God's hidden providence uses Esther and Mordecai to rescue the Jews (Purim), preserving the people through whom Messiah comes.
